title: break, continue, loop else clause

overview.why: Without break you must add a boolean flag to track whether a search succeeded. Without continue you must nest all your processing inside an if block. The loop else pattern eliminates an entire class of 'found it / not found' flag variables.
where_used: Searching for a matching config entry
Validating a list of permissions — stop on first failure or confirm all passed

why_learning_this: Eliminating flag variables (found = False ... if not found:) with the loop else clause
Writing clean early-exit search loops in FastAPI handlers and validation pipelines

common_mistakes.title: Thinking loop else means 'if the loop body never ran'
common_mistakes.explanation: The else clause runs when no break occurred — even if the loop executed zero iterations. It is about break, not about iteration count.

common_mistakes.title: Using break in a nested loop expecting it to exit the outer loop
common_mistakes.explanation: break only exits the innermost loop. To exit an outer loop, either restructure with a function and return, or use a flag variable.

common_mistakes.title: Using continue when an early return inside a function is cleaner
common_mistakes.explanation: If the loop body is a function, return at the top is often more readable than continue. Reserve continue for loops where you must keep iterating.

recall_questions.q: What does break do to a loop?
recall_questions.answer: It immediately exits the loop; remaining items are never visited.

recall_questions.q: What does continue do inside a loop body?
recall_questions.answer: It skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next item.

recall_questions.q: When does a loop's else clause run?
recall_questions.answer: Only when the loop completes without hitting a break — not when break was used to exit early.

recall_questions.q: What problem does the loop else clause solve cleanly?
recall_questions.answer: The 'search completed with no match found' pattern — it eliminates the need for a separate boolean flag variable.

practice_tasks.title: Find first active user
practice_tasks.prompt: Write first_active(users) that searches a list of user dicts for the first one where status == 'active'. Return that user dict, or None if none are active. Use break and the loop else clause — no flag variable.

code_walkthrough.focus: The loop checks all three routes without hitting break, so the else clause runs and prints '/admin not registered' — no flag variable needed.

challenge.title: Permission validator
challenge.prompt: Write check_permissions(required, granted) that iterates required permissions and uses continue to skip any already in granted, then prints 'missing: <perm>' for each absent one. After the loop, if every required permission was in granted (i.e., no 'missing' line was printed), use the loop else clause to print 'all permissions satisfied'. Test with a full match and a partial match.
